data: Inscriptions grecques dialectales de Sicile : contribution à l'étude du vocabulaire grec colonial
Laurent Dubois, Inscriptions grecques dialectales de Sicile : contribution à l’étude du vocabulaire grec colonial, CEFR 119 (Rome: Ecole française de Rome, 1989)
The text fills the face of the stone, with a margin of 10mm on the left edge, and a margin along the top of 22mm. The first two lines are larger than the rest.

Second or earlier first century BCE, primarily on palaeographic grounds (Manganaro various proposed dates between the end of 2nd century BCE and end of 1st century BCE (Manganaro 1963 and Cebeillac Gervasoni suggest post 36 BCE, assuming that line 17 implies the status of Centuripe as Latin municipium, which seems doubtful); Dubois, IGDS, suggests early 2nd century).

The text is set out in two columns on the front face. The left margin of the second column is justified, the right margin of the first column is uneven. Part of the text of the right column, from line 6 (the oath) onwards, is indented
Neat and regularly spaced letters (occasionally with a larger space between letters to mark a sense-break).

3rd — 2nd century BCE (Kaibel,
                                Consani 1996) (P-C sugg's contemp. with IG 211, 217)

Pugliese Carratelli, 1956, Silloge dell' epigrafi acrensi
Line 1 is on the cornice, with the rest of the text in two columns below

Line 1 is inscribed across the upper moulding (which is heavily damaged); the remainder of the text is engraved on the recessed front face, in two columns. The spacing between the columns after the first line is very irregular; column 2 has a half-line gap after line 2, meaning that the lines in the two columns are vertically out of synch after this point.

Neatly carved and regular letters, mostly without serifs, but some terminal wedges. Alpha with straight bar; epsilon on occasion has a slightly shorter mid-bar; zeta in form of I with wide horizontals; theta has short detached cross-bar; kappa has full-length arms; mu has vertical first and last, full length mid-strokes; xsi has three equal horiztonal bars; omicron is only slightly smaller than the other letters; pi has extended horizontal; rho has small rounded closed eye; sigma has horizontal top and bottom, with slihgtly variable mid-strokes; phi has small oval eye, with vertical often extended; omega is notably variable, always closed, but ranging from tear-drop to full circle on or even above and detached from the base line.
11-15 (with omicron down to 9 and phi up to 20)
Later third or second century BCE on palaeographic grounds.
Amati, 1827, Tavole greche de'magistrati annuali, scoperte ultimamente fra le rovine dell'antichissima città di Acre in Sicilia
The text is laid out in a single column, with a consistent left margin, with every second line indented by c.1 letter; the right margin is irregular.

Letters are plainly cut with minimal serifs and a square module. Alpha has straight bar; beta has equal closed loops; epsilon sometimes has equal length strokes, sometimes shorter middle bar; theta is full size, slightly elliptical, with plain cross-bar; kappa has full length arms; mu has vertical first and last, with middle strokes only half-descending; pi has overlapping horizontal, equal length vertical strokes; rho has closed eye of moderate size; sigma has horizontal upper and lower strokes; phi has an ovoid eye of varying size and extended vertical; omega varies between teardrop, rhomboid and small circle, always closed with horizontal baseline.
I.Sicily with the permission of the Assessorato Regionale dei Beni Culturali e dell’Identità Siciliana - Dipartimento dei Beni Culturali e dell’Identità Siciliana; photo J. Prag 2023-05-10.

Decree in honour of a gymnasiarch
The text, occupying 47 lines, fills the upper two-thirds of the main rectangular portion of the stele.

The lettering is tight, both in between letters and in interlinear spaces. The first two lines are larger and more spaced, though not necessarily more precise in execution. Alpha appears with both a straight and a curved crossbar. Interchangeable use of four-bar and lunate sigma, often in the same line, often close together. Interchangeable use of square and lunate epsilon, same variability and position as with sigma. There is occasional omission of the middle bar of a lunate epsilon, particularly in between two sigmas, resulting visually in the combination CCC. This occurs both at the beginning and end of lines, and does not seem to correspond to textual cues. Omega is mostly rhomboid or ovoid, occasionally round, though the few round instances seem to be an execution issue with the ovoid variant rather than a stylistic choice.
(Later) second or first century BCE (Dubois and Cordiano suggest 1st cent. BCE)

Three lines of Greek, beginning at the top of the front face, with the left margin consistent, right margin irregular.
Deeply incised letters without serifs. Broken bar alpha; eipsilon with shorter middle bar; small omicron above the line; Sigma somewhat open; smaller and open omega with short horizontal tails.
later third or earlier second century BCE on the basis of the letter forms.
I.Sicily with the permission of the Assessorato Regionale dei Beni Culturali e dell’Identità Siciliana - Dipartimento dei Beni Culturali e dell’Identità Siciliana; photo J.Prag 23-05-2022
Four lines of Greek letters centred on the face of the stone, but with a leftward bias.

Very neatly carved wide, regular letters of a fairly square module, with minimal serifs and all letters of near equal size. Straight bar alpha; epsilon with only slightly shorter middle bar; mu with vertical first and last, middle strokes descen half-way; omicron near full size; pi has equal length verticals with slight overhang on both sides of the cross bar; rho has large rounded closed eye; sigma has horizontal top and bottom strokes; phi is circular and without over-extended vertical; omega is open and tear-drop shaped.
late second century BCE, or early C1 BCE, following Ampolo and Erdas (previously dated earlier by Nenci) and confirmed by parallel material

Honours for an individual who undertook public building works
The text appears to be laid out with a more or less consistent left margin, vacats to the right.

Regularly cut letters with terminal serifs including horizontal caps to the tops of the alphas. Alpha has generally curved crossbar, sometimes equivalent to broken bar; epsilon has detached middle bar in the form of a triangle; theta has central point; kappa has full-length arms; omicron is full-size; pi has equal length verticals and overlapping horizontal; rho has a tapering eye; sigma has horizontal top and bottom, which overextend to left, the mid-strokes extending halfway, and straight; upsilon has straight arms; omega is tear-drop shaped, and closed, on a horizontal line.

The text is inscribed on three faces, the precise organisation of which remains a question of discussion. Battistoni 2020 and 2011, after Bormann, offers the most plausible and complete reconstruction, followed here: Section A in two columns on face I (left face for the viewer), continuing upper left on face III (central face for the viewer); Section B in a single column on face II (right face for the viewer); Section C, top of face III, followed by a large lacuna; Section D, lower left of face III; Section E.i, traces top right of face III, followed by a large lacuna; section E.ii, lower right of face III.

Inscription recording the neaniskoi of the gymnasium

The text fills the face available below the cornice (and seems likely to have continued below), with probably a consistent left margin, but a less regular right margin, and some cramping in order to fit words/phrases to the line (as e.g. in line 1).

The letters are mostly regular with a fairly square module and terminal points to the strokes rather than serifs. Alpha has broken cross-bar; epsilon has nearly equal bars; zeta in the form of an I with broad crossbars; kappa has short arms; mu has sloping outer strokes and half-height mid-strokes; omicron is nearly full-size, and consistently has a central dot (in lines 2 and 3 appears to have a line underneath); rho has rounded closed eye; sigma has horizontal top and bottom strokes, and the mid-strokes extend almost the full width; phi is standard height, elliptical; omega is consistently a closed circle (without the central dot of omicron), sometimes on the baseline, sometimes traversed by the line.
Photo J. Prag, 29.06.2016, with the permission of the Assessorato Regionale dei Beni Culturali e dell’Identità Siciliana - Dipartimento dei Beni Culturali e dell’Identità Siciliana
inscription recording the neaniskoi of the gymnasium

Wide, slightly irregular in size v-cut letters: alpha wide with straight bar; epsilon has shorter middle bar; omicron is full size; pi has equal length hastae; omega is rhomboid and almost closed; sigma has parallel top and bottom strokes, and slight tendency to curvature in line 2.
Photo J. Prag 02.03.2023, with the permission of the Assessorato Regionale dei Beni Culturali e dell’Identità Siciliana - Dipartimento dei Beni Culturali e dell’Identità Siciliana

Two lines of Greek letters, across the middle of the front face. The letters are aligned on the left margin, and are arranged in pseudo-stoichedon, i.e. vertically aligned.

Letters of the early Hellenistic or late classical period. Phi has an elliptical eye; alpha has straight bar; omicron is almost full size; sigma has widely diverging top and bottom strokes; theta is smaller than other letters, with a dot in the middle; omega is open; mu has diverging first and last strokes, and the mid-point only descends half-way; epsilon has short middle stroke. Words are separated on both lines by a three vertical points.
Photo J. Prag, courtesy Agrigento Museo Regionale Archeologico Pietro Griffo
Griffo, 1987, Il museo archeologico regionale di Agrigento
Sjöqvist, 1962, Excavations at Morgantina (Serra Orlando) 1961, Preliminary Report VI
Antonaccio, 1999, An inscribed stele from archaic Morgantina
Orsi suggested the text was probably originally centred on the rock, but only the right part of three lines now preserved.

Large letters, rendered somewhat crude and deep due to the coarse stone. Sigma is four-bar with slightly open upper and lower strokes; omega is large, rounded and open with short tails; alpha has straight bar; epsilon has shorter middle bar; nu has three full length strokes, first and last vertical; mu has vertical first and last, but shorter middle strokes descending only half-way; omicron is full-size; rho has fairly large rounded, closed eye.
Later 4th or 3rd century BCE on the basis of the letter forms

The lines of text begin at the left edge of the sheet (a margin of ca. 1-2 mm) and run across to the right edge leaving a right margin as narrow as ca. 1-2 mm (L.6) or as wide as ca. 7 mm (LL. 5, 9, 11): an effort was made to avoid ending a line in the middle of a word but this has happened at least between L. 1 and L. 2, and again between L. 8 and L. 9. Top margin ca. 1-2 mm; bottom margin unknown (support is broken along the entire bottom edge).
Manganaro, 1989, Case e terra a Kamarina e Morgantina nel III–II sec. a.C.

The text fills all the preserved surface; this appears to be a fragment from the center of the original lead sheet and no information about the margins or original extent of the text is preserved.
contract for the sale of property
Contract for the sale of a house and a shop

The left margin is not preserved but the first letters of most lines are preserved; the damage to the top has destroyed some of the text of L.1, and the ancient folds have damaged some of the text body. The right margin is entirely preserved and the bottom margin partially preserved.
contract for the sale of a house and a shop

Only the right margin of the text is preserved; the last preserved line of text may well be the final line of the original due to the lack of visible letters below it and the fact that the line runs closest to the right edge of the lamina (perhaps in an effort to avoid running onto a new line); the full bottom margin is not preserved. Top and left margins are unknown. No word breaks at line ends.
Contract for the mortgage of land and equipment

The left side of the text and its margin is completely preserved from L. 4 onward; all other margins are unknown. The text appears to wrap, e.g. from L. 4 to L. 5 where the break is between syllables.

Lines 1-3 are centred in the pediment. From line 4 onwards, the text is situated on the main rectangular body of the tablet. Lines 5-22 maintain a left-hand margin of one letter-space; line 4 begins hard against the left border fo the tablet. Line 23 (final line) is centred with larger letters (approximately double size).
First Punic War (264-241 BCE), and commonly attributed to the second half of the war.

Lines 1-3 are centred in the tympanon of the tablet. From line 4, the text is sited on the main rectangular body of the tablet. The left margin is ‘justified’, the right is not. The letters of line 27 are slightly taller and much wider and more widely spaced than the rest.

Entella 3 = Nakone A: decree of reconciliation

The text fills the face of the tablet. Lines 1-3 are indented by c.2 letters, and line 2 is surely complete and ended with a vacat occupied by the now lost nail hole upper right. Lines 34 and 35 appear compressed (implying the engraver was aware when he began line 34 that the text would run over; the vacat of line 35 is only partial, as line 34 falls towards the lower edge as it proceeds).

The several instances in which the cutter omitted one or more letters and subsequently inserted them above the already engraved text are notable (ll.8, 13, 20, 33); the phenomenon is not to be seen on the other tablets.
Image courtesy of the Centre for the Study of Ancient Documents, Faculty of Classics, University of Oxford

Entella 4 = B1, honours for Tiberius Claudius Antiatas

Entire text is sited on the main rectangular body of the tablet. The tympanon is occupied by a depiction of a triple-plumed helmet that intrudes down into lines 1-3, which it divides. The text maintains a left-hand margin of c.1 letter space, the left margin being ‘justified’, the right not. The empty space after the text is equivalent to 6 or 7 lines of text.
Ampolo, 2001, Da un'antica città di Sicilia. I decreti di Entella e Nakone
Souza, 2024, Entella: a resilient ancient Sicilian community
entella 4 = b1, honours for tiberius claudius antiatas
Entella 5 = A1, Entella thanks those who helped in the synoikismos

Lines 1-5 are roughly centred in the tympanon of the tablet. From line 6, the text is sited on the main rectangular body of the tablet. The left hand margin is ‘justified’, the right not. Line 15 exceeds the left hand margin by one letter space; the implication of both the position of the initial kappa and its form would seem to be that the engraver initially omitted it.
Lombardo, 1997, Il decreto V di Entella: note di lettura
entella 5 = a1, entella thanks those who helped in the synoikismos

Lines 1-3 are roughly centred in the tympanon (although note slight compression at end of line 3); Lines 4-22 occupy the main body of the tablet and the left margin is justified with a margin of c.1 letter space (with the exception of line 4, which is set hard to the left); the right margin is not justified. Line 23 is roughly centred, with larger letters.

Lines 1-5 fill the tympanon. Line 6 is engraved on the dividing strip, set to the left, avoiding the rivets. Lines 7-22 occupy the main body of the tablet and are left justified (but not right). The blank area at the foot of the tablet is equivalent to 3-4 lines of text.
